source.title: Hierarchical predictive coding in distributed pain circuits

confidence_rationale: Theoretical review framing pain as hierarchical predictive coding across a distributed cingulate-insula network. Argues for active inference with network-level coordination rather than either full decentralisation or single-site computation. Useful as architectural scaffolding for the SD-032 cluster and for reading the MECH-258 PE as a hierarchical residual, but not an empirical entry.
failure_signatures: A fully decentralised pain predictive-coding scheme (each region computes independently) is argued against.
A single-site computation (one region carries the whole forecast) is also argued against.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Pain perception is generated by a hierarchical predictive-coding scheme operating across distributed circuits, with the cingulate and insula serving as major hubs. Sub-regions of each have distinct functional roles. Active inference with traveling-wave oscillations is proposed as the integration mechanism.
mapping.ree_translation: Supports the SD-032 cluster architecture: multiple subdivisions each carrying part of the forecast, bound by a network-level coordinator. Favours a shared trunk with per-subdivision heads over fully independent per-subdivision predictors. For E2_harm_a vs E2_harm_s: both would be heads on a shared hierarchical predictor rather than two unrelated modules.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Theoretical framing, not empirical test. Contains no novel data. Useful for architectural defensibility but not for adjudicating between (a) and (b) on evidence.

confidence_components.notes: Theoretical review; weights less than empirical papers but provides the distributed-hierarchical framing that our SD-032 cluster already adopts.
